claim_chain: Prosocial behaviors require effort but people are reluctant to exert effort for others
Previous research has not examined how effort expenditure influences subsequent reward evaluation during prosocial acts
We found dissociable reward after-effects between self-benefiting and other-benefiting acts
Prior effort potentiated reward evaluation for self but attenuated it for others
This dissociation occurred specifically when reward magnitude was large
The dissociation was modulated by individual differences in effort discounting
These findings suggest distinct neural computations for self- and other-benefiting efforts

typical_pattern: State the main finding or topic sentence
Provide supporting evidence from the study data
Compare with or contrast to previous literature
Interpret the mechanism or theoretical explanation

likely_priorities: Mechanistic clarity of dissociable reward after-effects
Temporal confound between delay and effort level
Theoretical coherence across self and other conditions
likely_attacks: Temporal delay confounded with effort level
Limited to anonymous recipients rather than close others
Unclear whether findings generalize beyond physical effort
Potential alternative explanations for effort-discounting effect
Sample size or statistical power concerns
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly acknowledges 'One limitation of this study concerns the potential confound between temporal delay and effort level. Because higher effort levels required more time to complete, effort expenditure might not have directly affected subsequent reward'. The discussion also extensively addresses the need to 'manipulate the social distance of the recipient' and test whether 'the after-effect of effort expenditure could shift from an effort-discounting effect to an effort-enhancement effect when the beneficiary is a close other, such as a friend or family member'. The authors defend their theoretical interpretation by contrasting cognitive dissonance and psychological contrast theories with opportunity cost accounts, suggesting reviewers would scrutinize mechanistic explanations.

examples.quoted_phrase: our data suggest an effort-enhancement effect for self-benefiting acts but an effort-discounting effect for other-benefiting acts
This discounting effect may result from the heightened salience of opportunity costs in helping others
Our most important finding is the dissociation of the reward after-effect of effort expenditure between self-benefiting and other-benefiting trials
These results suggest that the dissociable effort after-effect relies on the involvement of the motivational system
Our findings reveal distinct neural computations for self- and other-benefiting efforts

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, author names, or invented numbers were included. The profile is based solely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.
